text:
CT CHEST / ABDOMEN / PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 63-year-old female with known metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma, status post right cytoreductive nephrectomy. Currently on nivo monotherapy following completion of four cycles of Yervoy/Opdivo induction. Prior restaging scan demonstrated heterogeneous behavior of pulmonary metastases with enlargement of the dominant right lower lobe lesion and decrease in smaller bilateral nodules; clinical concern for interval progression. This study obtained for confirmatory restaging assessment.

COMPARISON: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast, approximately 6 weeks prior (3-month restaging scan). CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast, approximately 6 months prior (pre-systemic therapy baseline, post-nephrectomy).

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis acquired following administration of 100 mL of Omnipaque 350 intravenous contrast using a standard oncologic protocol with portal venous phase. Oral contrast not administered. Multiplanar reformats reviewed.

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and pleura: There are several pulmonary nodules, most located in the lower lobes bilaterally and right middle lobe, as detailed below.

The dominant right lower lobe nodule (previously measured at 1.8 cm at baseline and 2.1 cm on the prior scan obtained approximately 6 weeks ago) now measures 2.6 cm in its greatest axial dimension. This lesion demonstrates further interval enlargement compared with both the prior and baseline studies, with an increase from 2.1 cm to 2.6 cm over the preceding 6-week interval -- representing approximately 24% growth from the most recent prior scan and substantially larger than at baseline. The lesion remains a solid pleural-based nodule in the posterior basal segment of the right lower lobe without surrounding ground-glass halo. There is no associated cavitation, pleural tethering, or satellite lesion formation.

A second right lower lobe nodule in the medial basal segment, previously measuring 0.9 cm at baseline and 0.7 cm on the prior scan, measures approximately 0.6 cm on today's study. This lesion appears grossly stable to minimally decreased over the interval.

The left lower lobe nodule in the posterior basal segment measures 0.5 cm, comparable to its measurement of 0.5 cm on the prior scan (0.7 cm at baseline). This lesion has been stable over the past two studies.

Two additional subcentimeter nodules were noted at baseline in the bilateral lower lobes. One right lower lobe subcentimeter nodule remains below 5 mm and is stable. A separate right lower lobe nodule that had measured approximately 0.5 cm on the prior scan is again seen at approximately 0.5 cm.

New findings in the lungs: There are two new nodules identified in the right middle lobe that were not present on the prior study or the original baseline imaging. The larger of the two measures approximately 0.6 cm and is located in the medial segment of the right middle lobe. A second nodule measures approximately 0.4 cm in the lateral segment of the right middle lobe. Both nodules are solid, well-circumscribed, and without surrounding infiltrate. These are new since baseline and were not present 6 weeks ago on the prior scan. In the clinical context of metastatic clear cell RCC, these are strongly suspicious for new metastatic deposits.

No additional new pulmonary nodules are identified. No pulmonary consolidation, ground-glass opacity, or interstitial abnormality to suggest immunotherapy-related pneumonitis. No pleural effusion bilaterally. No pneumothorax. Airways are patent to the subsegmental level.

Mediastinum and hila: The mediastinum is within normal limits for a post-right nephrectomy patient. No enlarged mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes. No pericardial effusion. Heart size normal. Aorta and great vessels unremarkable.

ABDOMEN AND PELVIS:

Surgical changes: Expected post-nephrectomy changes are again noted on the right. The right nephrectomy bed demonstrates stable postsurgical soft tissue stranding and fibrosis. No discrete soft tissue mass or nodule at the resection site to suggest local recurrence. The left kidney appears compensatorily hypertrophied, with preserved cortical thickness and no focal parenchymal lesion. No hydronephrosis on the left.

Liver: The liver is of normal size and morphology. No focal hepatic lesions are identified. No biliary ductal dilatation. Gallbladder is present and unremarkable. No pericholecystic fluid.

Pancreas: Pancreas is normal in appearance. No ductal dilatation, mass, or peripancreatic stranding.

Spleen: Normal in size and attenuation. No splenic lesions.

Adrenal glands: The right adrenal gland was resected at the time of nephrectomy. The left adrenal gland appears normal without thickening, nodularity, or mass lesion.

Retroperitoneum and lymph nodes: A new right paracaval lymph node is identified at the level of the mid-abdomen, measuring 1.1 x 0.7 cm in axial dimensions. This node was not identified on the prior scan (6 weeks ago) nor on the original pre-systemic therapy baseline imaging (6 months ago). The previously resected right hilar lymph node is of course absent. No other retroperitoneal lymph nodes meeting size criteria for pathologic enlargement. The aorta and inferior vena cava are patent without extrinsic compression.

Bowel: Small and large bowel loops are unremarkable without obstruction, wall thickening, or abnormal mucosal enhancement. No free intraperitoneal air or fluid.

Pelvis: Urinary bladder is partially distended and unremarkable without wall thickening. Uterus and adnexa are unremarkable for age. No pelvic lymphadenopathy. No free fluid.

Skeleton (imaged osseous structures): No suspicious lytic or sclerotic lesions are identified in the visualized axial or appendicular skeleton. No pathological fractures. Degenerative changes are noted in the lumbar spine consistent with the patient's age.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. The dominant right lower lobe posterior basal pulmonary metastasis has continued to enlarge, now measuring 2.6 cm compared to 2.1 cm on the prior study 6 weeks ago and 1.8 cm at the original baseline. This represents unequivocal interval growth over consecutive studies and is no longer attributable to pseudoprogression in the immunotherapy context.

2. Two new solid right middle lobe pulmonary nodules measuring 0.6 cm and 0.4 cm, not present at baseline or on the prior study, are new and highly suspicious for new metastatic deposits in this clinical setting.

3. A new right paracaval lymph node measuring 1.1 cm, not identified on prior imaging, is new and of concern for new nodal metastatic involvement.

4. The remaining bilateral lower lobe pulmonary nodules (right lower lobe medial basal segment and left lower lobe posterior basal segment) remain stable to slightly decreased, consistent with ongoing response in a subset of lesions.

5. No evidence of local recurrence at the right nephrectomy bed.

6. No hepatic, adrenal, or osseous metastases identified.

7. No pleural effusion. No pneumonitis.

Overall, the imaging pattern is one of disease progression with continued enlargement of the dominant pulmonary metastasis, two new right middle lobe pulmonary nodules, and a new right paracaval lymph node, occurring in the setting of stable or mildly responding smaller contralateral pulmonary lesions. The heterogeneous response pattern seen over the past two studies has now evolved into net progression with new site development. Clinical correlation with treatment response assessment is recommended.
